data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery: High Risk Of Unacceptable Bid
data.description:
Only a month or so ago, Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. (NASDAQ:WBD) traded at sub-$12, and now the stock is up over $21 based on a potential bidding war for their media assets. The major hiccup here is that the potential bidders are unlikely to meet the targeted goal of management. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock, especially after this big rally on an unlikely acceptable bid.

Stock Market Oddity

Warner Bros. has long struggled to shift into the video streaming wars. The company had the promising HBO service when streaming became hot, but a couple of merger distractions and poor execution left the business behind the leaders.

The media giant is now in the process of spinning off the cable networks, and the whole potential bid from Paramount Skydance (PSKY) and others only appears to be another major distraction at this point. CEO David Zaslav has long pointed to valuations above $30 as the true valuation of the stock.

Media reports suggest Paramount has apparently only bid $23.50 per share with a hard stop of $25 due to the cash requirements from Larry Ellison, father of CEO David Ellison. Charles Gasparino of Fox Business reports the Ellisons have already had 3 bids rejected.

The Paramount bid has always faced major headwinds due to the stretched funding requirements. Larry Ellison has a net worth topping $300 billion based on his massive position in Oracle (ORCL), but Paramount is only worth $18 billion, while Warner Bros. is worth $50 billion and would require upwards of $60 billion plus massive debt assumption to acquire.

The other potential bidders are Netflix (NFLX) and Comcast (CMCSA). The much larger media companies are expected to face more regulatory scrutiny, whether due to size or President Trump's concerns.

Besides, Netflix co-CEO Greg Peters has made several attempts to distance the streaming service from a major media buyout. While the executive was less dismal on the recent Q3'25 earnings call, Mr. Peters was clear that the company saw large media deals as constant failures back at the Bloomberg Screentime conference earlier this month, as follows:

We come from a deep heritage of being builders rather than buyers. One should have a reasonable amount of skepticism around big media mergers. They don’t have an amazing track record over time.

Back on the Q3'25 earnings call, the co-CEO outlined the major reasons media mergers don't work as follows:

So watching some of our competitors potentially to bigger via M&A does not change in and of itself, at least our view on the competitive landscape. And we don't think it changes the substance of the challenge that our competitors face. Specifically, the range of activities that we and our competitors have to get great at has never been assembled in a single company before.

Think about producing film and TV shows across multiple genres in multiple languages and dozens of countries around the world. I'm trying to figure out how to incorporate the latest technology, including AI and Gen AI. We're trying to figure out how we build better product experiences that can serve consumers better around the world. How about customer acquisition and retention? How do we optimize global payments? How do we optimize global partnerships? There is so much and we want to get better at all of those things. Our competitors are seeking to get better, all those things, of course, as well.

But you have to do that by the hard work of developing those capabilities in the trenches day to day. You don't get there simply by buying another company that is also still developing those same capabilities.

In essence, Netflix wants to focus on customer acquisition and how to incorporate AI into media to build the business, versus spending an incredible amount of time working with lawyers and regulators on closing a merger and integrating a complex business like Warner Bros. Regardless, though, Paramount appears to be heading towards making a hostile bid.

Tough Math

Paramount has the access to the cash for the deal, but the cash is outside of the business structure. A family member having money doesn't exactly help Paramount and shareholders extract any value from the Warner Bros. deal.

At $25 per share, Warner Bros. costs $62.5 billion, plus the company has a net debt load of $30.7 billion. The enterprise value reaches an incredible $93.2 billion, while again, Paramount has a merger stock valuation of only $18 billion.

The Ellisons would, in essence, pay a substantial amount of the deal price via cash for new shares, with a large portion via a loan. At the same time, Paramount would take on the $30 billion in net debt from WBD and still issue new shares for upwards of 20% of the deal.

The proposed deal value, based on Paramount Skydance trading at $16.50, was as follows, according to LightShed Partners:

  • $50 billion - 3 billion shares issued to Ellisons via $30 billion cash, $20 billion personal loan.
  • $30.7 billion - WBD debt assumed by Paramount.
  • $12 billion - 735 million shares issued to WBD shareholders.

The math gets incredibly more difficult for the Ellisons to spend an increasing amount of cash in order to bid $30 and beyond for the business. Each additional $5 bid is another $12.5 billion that has to be financed by the Ellisons or new debt by Paramount.

Warner Bros. is forecast to only produce around $8.5 billion in annual EBITDA, which leads to substantially lower actual profits. The deal supposedly offers $3 billion in synergies, but Netflix and research in our prior article highlight how these synergies don't lead to higher stock valuations.

WBD is already trading at close to 10x EV/EBITDA. The Ellisons are unlikely to pay a substantial additional amount personally in order to pay a richer valuation for the stock.

The risk remains upwards of $9 downside back to $12 on a bid failing to materialize, due in a big part to the practical financing issues with such a small business trying to swallow Warner Bros. The media reports suggest an upside potential of possibly $4 now, with a very low probability of the media company accepting a deal for only $25.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that the major risk is for WBD to not reach an acceptable deal, leading to the stock slumping. Investors should use the recent strength in the stock to unload a position.

data.title: Blackstone Q3: Distributable Earnings Up +48% (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I rate Blackstone Inc. (NYSE:BX) a buy for capital appreciation-focused investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments. After reaching a high of $190 in recent weeks, the stock dropped to $155 after the Q3 earnings call, representing an opportunity, in my view.

Over the past 10 years, Blackstone has outperformed the S&P500 (SP500)and the NASDAQ on a total return basis, growing to be the world’s leading alternative asset manager.

The recently posted Q3 results showed double-digit increases in fee-related and distributable earnings, which bodes well for its dividend policy. Assets under management (AUM) passed $1.2 trillion, confirming success in their investments and an ability to retain clients long term. The AUM is also an indication of future fee-related earnings, so the stock is on the right trajectory to grow for the rest of the year.

Finally, the company will benefit from recent legislation that allows some company retirement plans to include alternative assets, unlocking Blackstone’s funds to enter the $12 trillion market. President Trump signed an executive order in August allowing private equity and other alternatives to be included in 401(k) plans. Blackstone’s existing evergreen funds are ideally suited for the purpose.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are

  • Currently pays a quarterly dividend yielding 2.55% annually, going ex-dividend on 11/4/25.
  • BX has beaten the last 4 earnings estimates in a row.
  • Q3 earnings achieved double-digit increases in fee-related and distributable earnings.
  • Blackstone’s more than $1.2 trillion in assets under management include global investment strategies focused on real estate, private equity, credit, infrastructure, life sciences, growth equity, secondaries, and hedge funds.
  • Legislation allowing alternative assets in retirement accounts sets BX in a good position to grow.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Hold at 3.27 (as of 10/23/25). Note the key grades in Growth (A-) and Profitability (A-).

World’s Largest Alternative Asset Manager

Based in New York with over $1.2 trillion in AUM, Blackstone Inc. is an alternative asset management firm serving high-net-worth individuals and institutions. The company specializes in private equity, real estate, credit and insurance, infrastructure, life sciences, growth equity, secondaries, and hedge funds. It offers both public and private funds, such as Blackstone Real Estate Income Trust (BREIT) in which the company is the largest owner of commercial real estate. The company also provides capital markets services.

Q3 Earnings - Beat

On October 23, the company declared Q3 2025 earnings results, with the following financial highlights vs. prior year quarter:

  • Distributable earnings of $1.9 billion grew 48%.
  • Fee-related earnings (FRE) of $1.5 billion grew 26%.
  • Revenues of $3.09 billion were down -15.7%
  • Expenses declined -7.7% to $1.75 billion.
  • Net Income of $1.2 billion declined -20.94%
  • Net Income Attributable to Blackstone Inc. was $624.9 million, down -20.0%
  • Repurchased 200k shares in the quarter.
  • Distributed $1.6 billion in dividends to shareholders.

Overall, Blackstone posted outstanding growth in earnings, which follows the previous three quarters in exceeding estimates. In particular, the distributable earnings growth is an important metric to watch as it covers the dividend.

Blackstone’s AUM grew +12% to $1.24 trillion, as seen in the chart below. This is critical in my view, as it shows an indication of future fee-related earnings and proof of performance. The company is growing its clients' assets, and the clients are sticking with them. Perpetual capital reached $500.6 billion (or half a trillion), an increase of 15% year over year.

Performance To Date

An investment of $10,000 in BX over the past 10 years would have grown to $75,820, outperforming the financial sector index as represented by Sector SPDR Fund (XLF), which would have made $32,720. In fact, BX surpassed the S&P500 (SPY) and the NASDAQ market (QQQ).

The extraordinary growth is due to a number of factors, including the ability of the company to attract investors, performance in high return sectors such as private equity, and trust in management, who are global leaders in their field. Blackstone serves large institutional investors who commit large pools of capital, such as pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, and ultra-high-net-worth investors. Blackstone has carved out a reputation for having smart people on the team who deliver results ahead of peers. This outperformance has earned the stock a premium with expectations of continuing to beat the estimates and grow at a higher rate than the rest of the industry.

Valuation

As an asset manager, BX pays the dividend based on distributable earnings made in the quarter, not trying to keep the dividend at a certain rate. Therefore, the distributions are variable, as seen in the chart below. Overall, the trend shows an increasing dividend. In my view, BX is not a stock for income investors, but rather for growth, or capital appreciation over time.

Overall, the dividend distribution is growing as the company expands. For example, in 2014 the stock paid $1.88 per share, and is now expected to pay $4.12 per share in 2025.

In comparison to peers, BX is the largest asset manager by market capitalization, as seen in the chart below. The dividend at 2.6% is average, but the dividend growth rate of 14.8% is the best amongst peers. BX’s 10-year total return of 626% is the second highest within the group. Return on equity of 29% is the highest amongst peers. Given its leadership, it's not surprising, in my view, that its price-to-book ratio of 15.2 is at a premium to peers.

BlackRock, Inc. (BLK) is second behind BX in market cap but has the ability to quickly pass BX based on growth of ETFs, in my view. BLK shares similar impressive metrics, such as an 8% 5-year dividend growth rate, 14% ROE, and 331% 10-year total return.

KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) distributes a tiny dividend, focused more on capital appreciation, with the highest 5- and 10-year total return amongst peers.

Brookfield Corporation (BN), similar to KKR, barely has a dividend but delivers strong 5- and 10-year total returns. Its return on equity (ROE) is the lowest of the group at 1.8%.

Brookfield Asset Management Ltd. (BAM) distributes the highest dividend at 3.2% and a high ROE of 21%.

The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation (BK) delivers strong metrics, including a 2% dividend and 230% 5-year total return.

Over the past 10 years, BX grew 658% on a total return basis, edged out only by KKR with a 771% total return. Each one of these companies is far above peers. One of the takeaways, in my opinion, is that size and scale matter in these financial companies. Also, the profitability of alternative investments such as private equity generates more value than public securities.

Outlook

Blackstone is well situated to continue the growth of its AUM under the current bull market, which will be further fueled by the expected rate cut at the end of October. Layering on the expected sell-in of alternatives into retirement accounts, Blackstone is expected to continue its winning run into 2026.

Risks To Rating

Under the current macroeconomic situation, highly valued stocks are at risk for profit taking if the market sells off, such as during a correction. Many guest speakers within the financial media are talking about an AI “stock bubble,” adding to the “wall of worry” about what might trigger a sell off. For example, recently President Trump triggered a sharp sell-off with a social media post expressing his displeasure with China about the tariff negotiations.

Bottom Line

I rate Blackstone – BX a Buy given the recent drop in price after announcing better-than-expected 3Q results. After trading at a high of $190, the stock dropped to $155, presenting an opportunity, in my experience. BX currently pays a dividend yielding 2.55%, going ex-dividend on November 4th.

Over the past 10 years, BX has outperformed all key stock indexes and most peers, achieving a 626% total return. Growth of AUM to $1.2 trillion has made it the world's largest alternative asset manager.

Going forward, BX is driven by the bull market and has strong potential to expand into retirement accounts such as 401(k) programs. Recent legislation passed by executive order in August, allows some companies to offer alternative investments, such as Blackstone’s evergreen funds, in company retirement plans. This represents a large opportunity given the $12 trillion retirement market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BUZZ Investing: BUZZ Index Surges As Fed Cut And AI Leaders Drive Gains
data.description:
Investors cheered a dovish Fed pivot and resilient tech earnings as U.S. equities extended gains, with retail-fueled sentiment lifting select high-growth names.

U.S. equities posted further gains during the recent period between index selection dates (September 11, 2025 – October 9, 2025, the “Period”), extending their late-summer momentum and pushing major benchmarks to new highs. The S&P 500 rose and the Nasdaq Composite outperformed, supported by optimism surrounding the Federal Reserve’s long-anticipated 25-basis-point rate cut in mid-September. While the decision was widely expected, the accompanying statement struck a notably dovish tone, with policymakers signaling that additional easing may be appropriate should labor conditions continue to soften. Large-cap technology stocks led performance once again, buoyed by resilient earnings and continued enthusiasm around artificial intelligence. Broader markets benefited from the perception that policy is now pivoting toward supporting growth, helping sustain the rally despite lingering geopolitical and trade-related uncertainty. The BUZZ NextGen AI US Sentiment Leaders Index (the “BUZZ Index”) returned 17.4% during the Period versus 2.3% for the S&P 500. Year to date, the BUZZ Index has risen 57.9%, compared to the S&P’s 15.7%.

Monetary policy developments loomed large. On September 17, the Fed delivered a 25 basis point rate cut to bring its benchmark to 4.00-4.25%, citing rising downside risks to employment and affirming expectations for more cuts this year. The statement noted that downside risks to the labor market have increased. Treasury yields broadly fell on the news. Labor data remained soft: the August payrolls miss and rising unemployment had already stoked easing expectations; the weak report still appeared to validate the Fed’s shift. Inflation data showed mixed signals, with core measures remaining sticky even as headline prints flirted with moderation. Tariff risk re-entered the equation, notably with new duties announced on lumber and furniture, complicating the outlook for goods inflation. Overall, the Period was defined by equities pressing higher under the tailwind of monetary easing, while macro crosscurrents around inflation and trade reminded markets that the path ahead may not be entirely smooth.

The BUZZ Index returned 12.14% during the month of September compared to a return of 3.65% for the S&P 500 Index during the same period. Year-to-date, the BUZZ Index leads the S&P 500 with returns of 45.21% and 14.83%, respectively, as of the end of September.

AST SpaceMobile and Intel Lead BUZZ Performance on Landmark Partnership Announcements

Shares of AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) surged 126.2% during the Period, leading contributors to BUZZ Index performance. The move followed the October 8 announcement of a definitive commercial agreement with Verizon (VZ) to offer direct-to-cell service to Verizon customers beginning in 2026, leveraging Verizon’s 850 MHz spectrum alongside ASTS’s space-based network. The deal built on successful voice, video, and messaging demonstrations using unmodified smartphones and was widely viewed as a milestone toward commercial rollout, helping re-rate growth expectations for the space-to-cell category.

Intel (INTC) rose 53.6% during the Period, supported by a series of high-profile announcements that may have reshaped the company’s AI and PC road map. On September 18, NVIDIA (NVDA) disclosed a $5 billion investment in Intel stock alongside a multi-year collaboration to co-develop data-center and PC products, including x86 system-on-chips integrating RTX GPU chiplets and deeper NVLink connectivity. The partnership was interpreted as a notable vote of confidence in Intel’s technology stack and potential role in next-generation AI platforms. Momentum may have also reflected the U.S. government’s previously announced 9.9% equity stake in Intel in late August, which aimed to bolster domestic semiconductor capacity and added to the sense of balance-sheet support heading into the fall.

Rocket and DraftKings Pull Back; Execution Risks and New Competition Pressure Shares

Shares of Rocket Companies (RKT) declined during the Period, ranking among the leading detractors to BUZZ Index performance. The weakness followed the completion of its acquisition of Mr. Cooper Group (COOP) on October 1, a transformative deal that created the largest mortgage originator and servicer in the U.S. While strategically compelling, investors may have been cautious about near-term integration risks, potential margin dilution, and execution complexity. Index rebalancing around the close added further technical pressure, as S&P Dow Jones Indices announced the removal of Mr. Cooper from the S&P SmallCap 600 and the inclusion of Western Union in its place. The shift, combined with subdued housing-market sentiment and persistent interest-rate uncertainty, may have contributed to selling pressure even as the combined platform positions Rocket for long-term scale advantages.

DraftKings (DKNG) was another notable detractor, retreating amid growing concerns over new forms of competition in online wagering. Shares fell sharply in late September after reports highlighted surging trading volumes on federally regulated prediction-market platforms such as Kalshi and Polymarket, which allow users to trade event-based contracts outside traditional state-regulated sports-betting frameworks. The platforms’ nationwide accessibility and favorable tax treatment drew attention from investors who viewed them as potential disruptors to the sports-betting duopoly of DraftKings and FanDuel. The decline erased much of the stock’s summer gains, as traders reassessed near-term growth prospects despite DraftKings’ continued expansion in legalized markets and steady progress toward profitability.

BUZZ Index October 2025 Rebalance Highlights

New Entrants to the BUZZ Index

This month, the BUZZ Index welcomes several first-time entrants; many tied to high-profile themes dominating market and social media discussions. IREN Limited (IREN) and Applied Digital Corporation (APLD) highlight the continued enthusiasm surrounding AI infrastructure and data center development. Rigetti Computing, Inc. (RGTI) and D-Wave Quantum Inc. (QBTS), both leaders in quantum computing, have also gained attention as optimism builds around the path toward commercial adoption of quantum technologies. Shares of QuantumScape Corporation (QS), which operates in the lithium battery space, have quadrupled since June amid renewed interest in next-generation energy storage. Collectively, these stocks reflect strong price momentum and elevated investor engagement. The exception is Webull Corporation (BULL), the online trading platform that went public in April through its merger with SPAC SK Growth Opportunities Corp. After an early surge which saw its shares rally from $11 to an intra-day high of near $80, the stock quickly retraced to the $11 range and has since mostly traded sideways. Sentiment, however, had remained positive, buoyed by favorable comparisons to Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD), which has gained more than fourfold since spring. BULL enters the Index as the third-largest new addition this month, with a 2.87% weight.

Opendoor Technologies Inc.

Amid this month’s wave of new entrants to the Index, none has drawn more attention than Opendoor Technologies (OPEN). The stock’s remarkable rally has evoked comparisons to the early days of the meme-stock era led by GameStop. Between July 1 and July 21, shares surged from $0.50 to $5.00, driven by a rapidly expanding online community of retail investors. Much of the renewed enthusiasm centered on Toronto hedge fund manager Eric Jackson, whose unconventional campaign to spotlight Opendoor’s “value” potential, including daily appearances outside rapper Drake’s Toronto residence holding a sign for the “$OPEN Army”, went viral across social media. Following an initial pullback to $2, the stock extended its advance through August and September, reaching over $10 per share before drifting lower. Sentiment across online platforms remains exceptionally strong, underscoring sustained retail engagement and optimism surrounding the company’s recovery narrative. This month, OPEN holds the highest positive sentiment score in the eligible universe and enters the BUZZ Index at the maximum 3% weight.

data.title: The Hidden Weakness In The Jobs Market Could Cause A Major Market Disruption
data.description:
The U.S. jobs market has been rattled recently by rising unemployment. In early 2024, it stood at around 3.9%, and since then, it has risen fairly steadily to a 4.3% level today.

While the trend is not good, when zooming out and looking at the full historical spectrum of U.S. unemployment rates, the current number actually looks quite mild, showing that the U.S. labor market by historical standards is still quite strong.

However, despite the headline number indicating a fairly stable jobs market, underneath the surface, there are numerous indicators showing that it is deteriorating, which could have some major negative impacts on the broader economy and, by extension, the stock market (SP500). In this article, we will dig into this and share our outlook for the U.S. economy and market, as well as how investors should be thinking about allocating capital in this environment.

Cracks Beneath The Surface

One of the biggest challenges in getting a good understanding of the jobs market is the fact that the U.S. government has been technically shut down for several weeks now, which has halted many official data releases, forcing economists to look to other places for insight into the health of the labor market. One indicator that is likely to deal a blow to the jobs market, at least in the near term, is that almost 100,000 federal employees formally came off the payroll due to their deferred resignations finally being processed, thereby becoming one of the largest one-week reductions in the U.S. workforce in history.

Another source that economists can look to is the Chicago Fed’s dashboard, which shows unemployment ticking up by a single basis point from 4.33% in August to 4.34% in September. Meanwhile, the hiring rate fell to 45.2%, which is its lowest level since 2009, and the layoff rate held constant at 2.1%, which was relatively reassuring and indicates that companies are not firing aggressively. However, the falling hiring rate means that companies are allowing natural attrition to take hold by reducing the new employees they are bringing in, even if they are not removing existing ones quite as rapidly.

Additionally, another resource (the Challenger report) indicated that there have been 946,426 job cuts announced so far this year, which is 24% higher than the full year of 2024, which is quite alarming. New hires are down a whopping 58% year-over-year, the weakest pace of new hiring since the Great Financial Crisis. Challenger’s senior vice president connected this degree of job cuts with recessions, although he also admitted that it could be due to an early wave of automation through AI and robotics in the manufacturing and tech fields. Alternatively, it could be a combination of the two. Either way, it indicates that the job market is beginning to weaken in a major way.

Government Cuts Add To The Strain

One potential wild card here is that the government is shedding a sizable portion of its workforce, which is contributing significantly to the overall labor market weakness. About 154,000 government employees accepted buyouts through the deferred resignation offer, and 100,000 of them officially exited the workforce in early October. One downside emphasized by Oxford Economists and Wells Fargo (WFC) is that these are some of the most stable jobs, even during economic downturns. This could lead to a weakening of consumer demand in the event that we go through a recession, making it harder for the economy to find a bottom. On the flip side, government jobs are often among the least productive and are ultimately funded by taxation and/or inflation through money printing. So, while cutting these jobs may weaken the economy in the near term, over the long term, it could quite possibly have a net positive impact on the economy.

Nonetheless, if the private sector is already being hit by the rise of AI and automation while the economy is also weakening, this could be an untimely hit that could push the economy and ultimately the labor market into a very dark place. As WFC recently said:

while the jobs market is not unraveling, it continues to teeter on a knife's edge. Strengthening in end-demand could unleash pent-up demand for workers, but a further moderation or negative shock to growth could tip the scales the other direction, setting off a negative feedback loop between income, spending and hiring

Other important factors to keep in mind are that the Fed is now cutting rates in an attempt to prop up the labor market, and there is a large stimulus being provided by the massive AI CapEx boom, which is likely not sustainable for an extended period of time. These tailwinds in many respects offset the headwind to the jobs market being provided by government layoffs.

Hidden Weakness In The Numbers

That said, there are some other indicators that the jobs market could be on a weak footing. In particular, the unemployment rate is being suppressed because the participation rate in the labor force has been falling and underemployment has been rising. This makes the jobs market appear stronger than it really is. Additionally, unemployment is expected to rise to 4.5% next year, with monthly job creation falling to 29,000 versus 87,000 previously. Core PCE, a key inflation indicator, is seen sitting at 2.5% at the end of 2026, which leaves it still 50 basis points above the Fed’s 2% target. If that indeed plays out, it could limit the Fed’s ability to prop up the jobs market through interest rate cuts and quantitative easing.

Ultimately, the U.S. jobs market is being propped up by heavy deficit spending by the U.S. government and accommodative monetary policy. At the same time, however, federal layoffs and the hiring freeze are taking a bite out of a key segment of consumer demand, which in many ways props up the economy and helps it find a bottom during economic downturns. Meanwhile, households are experiencing record levels of debt and a rising number of delinquencies, which, along with the AI CapEx boom, are providing unsustainable support to the economy and, by extension, the jobs market. With inflation remaining at an elevated level, it also limits the Fed’s ability to act aggressively to support the jobs market during an economic downturn.

What It Means For The Market

What does this mean for the market? It means that there are likely signs lurking beneath the surface that indicate the jobs market is on shaky footing and unemployment could easily balloon higher, especially if the trade war between the U.S. and China flares up again, as it potentially could depending on how President Trump and Chairman Xi’s meeting (and the implementation of any potential agreement) goes next week. By extension, if the jobs market weakens significantly, consumer demand will likely plummet as over-indebted households will soon see delinquencies soar even higher if many of them lose their jobs. This would have a follow-on effect of hurting many businesses that are also heavily indebted, leading to a rise in corporate defaults, commercial real estate (VNQ) vacancies, and, by extension, commercial real estate loan losses.

Given that the S&P 500 is already significantly overvalued based on numerous valuation models—including the Buffett Indicator, the price-to-earnings model, the price-to-sales model, and the mean reversion model—it likely means that there will be significant downside ahead for the market if this plays out. Given the growing risks to the labor market, the complacency surrounding the market, as indicated by its nosebleed valuation and position near all-time highs, seems irrational to me. Therefore, I am steering clear of broad index funds like the S&P 500, Nasdaq (NASDAQ:QQQ), and Dow Jones Industrial Average (NYSEARCA:DIA), and instead allocating capital to undervalued, defensively positioned dividend stocks (SCHD) in niche sectors that are significantly undervalued right now. I am also complementing those stocks with an overweight position in gold (GLD) and silver (SLV) bullion leases that generate attractive passive income while also serving as a hedge against geopolitical and macroeconomic turmoil. This is the approach that we are taking in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: 9.2% Dividend Yield With Seniority From Annaly Capital
data.description:
When it comes to mortgage REITs, Annaly Capital Management (NLY) is a name most investors will know. They’re at the top and have performed well with everything that is going on. However, there are a couple of major issues with investing in the common stock.

  1. For investors who are risk-averse, mortgage REITs are generally not a great option. They carry material risk and should be considered trading securities. We regularly trade in and out of mortgage REITs based on their trading relative to book value. Mortgage REIT common shares are not securities that fit into a portfolio where investors would just like to buy shares and then sit on them for a significant amount of time.

  2. Over the last several months, Annaly has climbed substantially. Even if investors were interested in the common stock, we view shares as being significantly overpriced.

Do Investors Have Another Choice?

Investors who are interested in the mortgage REIT sector should be looking at preferred shares in the sector. Preferred shares are great for risk-averse investors and also for investors who are willing to take on more risk but demand returns for the risk. Annaly Capital has several preferred shares. Anyone who has followed us for years knows that we love buying into NLY preferred shares when the price is right. Currently, NLY-F is hovering near a buy rating:

NLY-F is about $0.16 above our buy rating. Our target price takes call risk into account. The most notable risk right now would be Annaly Capital calling shares of NLY-F as soon as you purchase shares. Investors would stand to lose about $0.15 per share if the company were to call shares instantly after you purchased them. While this is a risk, it’s nowhere near a 100% chance that shares are called. If they do not call shares, then investors get to continue collecting a big dividend. The amount of the dividend will probably dip next quarter based on the latest forecasting for the Fed Funds Rate. The Federal Reserve’s action with the Fed Funds Rate drives other short-term rates, which ends up impacting the dividend rate.

If you expect the Federal Reserve to cut rates several times, you’ll probably choose to pass on these. If you only expect another 1 to 2 rate cuts, you may find them quite attractive.

Coverage

For every preferred share (or baby bond) we cover, we track the common-equity-to-preferred-share liquidation ratio. If we had a “worst-case” scenario and the company had to liquidate, the company would be required to pay the preferred shareholders before the common shareholders. Therefore, the more common equity they have relative to preferred equity, the more cushion exists. NLY has one of the best ratios in the sector at 6.81, which rises to 7.31 if we use market cap to preferred share value.

We like to look at the ratio as common equity to preferred equity because in the event of a liquidation, then the stock price has a very significant chance of dropping.

We include market cap to preferred share value because it helps to recognize if a mortgage REIT is trading above or below book value. If they trade at a large premium, they are more likely to issue shares. If they trade at a large discount, investors are treating it as if they are skeptical of the book value.

Annaly currently has several preferred shares, and investors should be looking at all of them. One of the reasons call risk is a real risk here is that if Annaly decided to call preferred shares, it would be NLY-F and NLY-I that would be the targets. The other preferred shares are paying out lower dividends based on the call price of $25.

I’m talking about NLY-F in this article, but you can apply most of the concepts to NLY-I. NLY-G pays out materially less. Annaly has used preferred shares for a long time.

NLY-J Comparison

NLY-J is also a viable choice for investors. The yield is comparable, and the fixed rate could be nice if short-term rates continue to fall. However, NLY-J trades at a material premium to the $25 base value for the shares. We estimate about $.68 of that is dividend accrual, so at $25.98, the stripped price on NLY-J is about $25.30. Still, many investors may not feel comfortable with the extra premium to call value. Either way, it’s worth mentioning in the article.

Final Thoughts

Annaly’s common shares have already had a strong run, and that limits the upside from the common stocks. Mortgage REITs can still have a place in an active trader’s portfolio, but they’re not the kind of position you would want to buy and hold. For most investors, the approach that makes more sense for long-term positions is looking at the preferred shares. They offer solid income and far less volatility than the common stock.

Among Annaly’s preferred shares, NLY-F is one of the closest to a buy rating. Prices aren’t quite cheap enough to rush in today, but they’re close. When (or if) NLY-F dips back into our buy range, we’ll be considering buying. That’s how we handle our investments - wait for the right price and stay disciplined. After that, each investor needs to decide what makes sense for their risk tolerance.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: IT Outage Grounds Alaska Fleet Again
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Alaska Airlines (ALK) IT outage halts flights. (00:30) Target (TGT) cuts 1,800 corporate job positions in significant downsizing. (01:39) All eyes on CPI report after weeks of economic data blackout. (02:20)

This is an abridged transcript.

Operations at Alaska Airlines (NYSE:ALK) have been restored after the airline grounded all its flights on Thursday after an IT outage impacted operations.

Just before 4:30pm Pacific time, Alaska Airlines sent a tweet on X that there was a temporary ground stop in place and that passengers should check their flight status before going to the airport.

The airline sent another update on X after midnight Pacific time that operations had been restored and the ground stop had been lifted at 11:30 pm.

The company warned that more than 220 flights had been cancelled and that more flight disruptions were likely.

Passengers on Alaska and Horizon should check the flight status before going to the airport.

This is the second IT outage this year. The last incident was in July.

Due to the outage, Alaska Airlines also announced that it has postponed its conference call for third-quarter 2025 financial results. The call was scheduled for today at 11:30am Eastern time.

Target Corporation (NYSE:TGT) disclosed on Thursday that it will cut 1,800 positions from its corporate headcount. The specific employees being let go will not be notified until next Tuesday.

The restructuring will include 1,000 employees being let go and the removal of roughly 800 open positions, which is about 8% of Target's (TGT) global team. Notably, U.S. corporate staff have been asked to work remotely next week while the notification process plays out.

The Minneapolis-based retailer said changes were aimed at making Target's (TGT) operations more agile.

Shares of Target (TGT) are 0.7% higher in premarket trading.

Investors will finally get some official economic data from the government, with the Consumer Price Index report due for release this morning, even as the federal shutdown continues to drag on.

Economists largely expect persistent retail inflation in September, increasing at the same pace as the month before.

Headline CPI in September is seen rising 0.4% M/M, same as the prior month. On a Y/Y basis, CPI is expected to rise 3.1%, compared with +2.9% in August.

Core CPI, which excludes food and energy prices, is projected to rise 0.3% M/M in September and 3.1% Y/Y - the same increases seen in August.

The CPI report was scheduled for Oct. 15, but the Bureau of Labor Statistics pushed it to Oct. 24 due to the government shutdown.

The data will be the last significant economic reading before the Federal Reserve's monetary policy meeting next week.

CPI will be released at 8:30am.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Trump cancels trade negotiations with Canada over Reagan ad

Intel perks up as PC, AI helps boost Q3 results, guidance

Rivian reaches $250M settlement to resolve investor lawsuit over price hikes

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is flat at $61/barrel. Bitcoin is up 1.1% at $111,000. Gold is down 0.3% at $4,070.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.1% and the DAX is down 0.1%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) +8% - Shares gained after the company delivered a stronger-than-expected Q3, with both earnings and guidance beating Wall Street estimates.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am CPI

  • 10:00 am Consumer Sentiment

It's Friday so that means it's also news quiz day. Test your knowledge of what happened this week. I’ll leave a link to the quiz in the show notes section.

data.title: Allegion: Turning Out To Be A Real Secure Play
data.description:
In July, I believed that continued M&A efforts added to the track record of Allegion (NYSE:ALLE). A steady bolt-on M&A strategy added to the growth profile, as modest leverage and a reasonable 19 times earnings multiple looked quite fair, to slightly appealing.

Since the summer, shares have risen some 17%, vastly outpacing market returns, aided by continued operating momentum and more deals being announced. This operating momentum has been accompanied by a strong share price performance, pushing up the valuation multiples a bit further.

While the strong business performance is impressive, I am not willing to chase shares here.

All About Security

Allegion provides security and access solutions, with the business having a mission to create both a safer and more accessible world. The company employs about 12,000 workers which develop, manufacture, and sell such products, marketed across 30 brands. These include names like AXA, Kryptonite, Bricard, and Schlage, among others.

The company generates some $3.8 billion in sales in 2024, with EBITDA margins reported at nearly 25% of sales. About two-thirds of revenues were derived from mechanical solutions, complemented by electronic security solutions and, to a smaller extent, software & services. Typical products include mechanical locks, e-locks, closers, automatic doors, frames, and hinges.

The company generates about 80% of sales in the Americas, complemented by operations abroad, which are not just smaller in terms of sales, but also lag in terms of margins. The company has a long-term model that calls for a compelling 7% revenue growth, built up pretty evenly between pricing, organic volumes, and M&A contribution.

Over the past decade, the company has largely delivered on this, having nearly doubled sales over the past decade, as operating margins have been creeping up to levels in the low twenties. These are solid achievements, certainly, as we recognize that the company has bought back about one in every ten shares over this period of time.

Valuation Thoughts

Early in 2024, the company reported a 3% increase in 2024 sales to $3.77 billion, with adjusted earnings up 7% to $7.53 per share, this number looking quite clean. The company guided for a modest 1-3% increase in 2025 sales, with adjusted earnings seen up modestly to $7.75 per share.

Net debt of $1.50 billion was relatively reasonable, with EBITDA reported just shy of $900 million, for a leverage ratio around 1.7 times. After a solid 5% increase in first-quarter sales, the company announced a substantial EUR 330 million deal for German-based ELATEC. That purchase did not come cheap, given a EUR 60-65 million revenue contribution, suggesting that over a 5 times sales multiple was paid.

Trading at $148 in July, the company was set to operate with a pro forma net debt load of $1.9 billion, with leverage ratios seen in the low 2s. Based on the $7.75 per share earnings guidance, the company was valued at 19 times earnings, being reasonable multiples given the momentum displayed by the business.

Doing Well

Over the summer, shares have risen some 20 dollars to $168 per share, as the company has continued to display solid momentum here. In July, the company announced an apparent bolt-on deal with the purchase of Gatewise Incorporated, a smart access control solutions provider, with no financial details announced on the purchase.

The company furthermore announced another bolt-on deal, with the purchase of Waitwhile, a SaaS provider focusing on schedule and queue management. In the second quarter earnings presentation, the combined purchase price of these two deals was announced at $80 million.

By the end of the month, the company reported its second quarter results. Revenues rose by nearly 6% to $1.02 billion, as organic growth topped 3%. Quarterly adjusted earnings rose by eight cents to $2.04 per share. Net debt was reported at $1.41 billion, which is ahead of the ELATEC deal closing, making that leverage is seen around 2 times pro forma ELATEC closing over the summer.

Subsequently, the company hiked the full year sales guidance, now seeing sales up 7%, with adjusted earnings seen up to $8.00-$8.15 per share. This is aided by dollar weakness and the contribution from recent M&A efforts. Of course, earnings are coming in stronger than anticipated, yet with shares advancing, earnings multiples have expanded to 21-22 times earnings.

Further deals were announced in August, as the company announced the purchase of UK-based security solution provider UAP, with, again, no financial details being announced. This was followed by the purchase of Brisant as well later that month, the second UK-based acquisition that month.

Third quarter results were released recently, and the results look quite decent, with reported revenues up more than 10% and organic growth reported near 6%. Somewhat disappointing in this respect is that adjusted earnings per share were up just over 6% to $2.30 per share. Alongside the results, the company hiked the full-year earnings guidance to $8.10-$8.20 per share. Adjusted EBITDA now trends at a billion per annum, perfectly manageable with net debt reported at $1.78 billion. With operating margins lagging a bit, investors were not too happy with lagging earnings growth, as shares traded down by around 4% in response to the results, albeit after expectations had been creeping up over time.

Hence, I look forward to substantial dips, as I am growing more comfortable with the operating performance of Allegion here.

What Now?

The reality is that I am impressed by the strong track record of the business, as the company has been stepping up M&A efforts in recent months. This comes with still very reasonable leverage, as momentum is very strong, yet this has been recognized in the shares as well.

Consequently, shares have enjoyed a bit of valuation multiple inflation, with the earnings multiples having increased two turns since July, to a relatively reasonable 20-21 times earnings here. Right now, I am reserved given the pricing action, but keen to revisit shares on dips.

data.title: Introducing Global Ex-U.S. Quality Growth
data.description:
By Matt Wagner, CFA

The WisdomTree Global ex-U.S. Quality Growth Fund (DNL) is evolving.

Reflecting its broadened universe, the Fund is dropping "Dividend" from its name and introducing enhancements to its methodology designed to better capture global quality growth opportunities.

Effective October 23, the WisdomTree Global ex-U.S. Quality Growth Fund—which tracks the similarly renamed WisdomTree Global ex-U.S. Quality Growth Index—will broaden its scope to include non-dividend-paying companies and shift its weighting approach from dividend-based to market capitalization.

Methodology Changes

DNL has long been the global ex-U.S. counterpart to the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth Fund (DGRW).

Historically, the dividend requirement has shifted DGRW toward value.

Internationally, the dividend requirement is a less restrictive filter on a starting investment universe, as there are fewer mega-cap non-dividend payers like Amazon, Tesla or Netflix. Consequently, DNL's profitability and growth screens naturally tilted it toward growth characteristics, creating a mismatch with the more value-oriented U.S. approach.

To better capture the growth segment DNL was already aligned with, and create an international complement to the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Fund (QGRW), the updated Index now:

  • Removes the dividend requirement, expanding the investable universe
  • Selects the top 200 companies (previously 300) ranked by combined growth and quality metrics
  • Weights by market capitalization rather than cash dividends
  • Rebalances semiannually (end of March and September screening) instead of annually
  • Includes an Index committee as an additional layer of oversight

Figure 1: Index Methodology Change Summary

Top Holdings Changes

Five of the prior top 10 holdings were dropped, replaced by four new entrants. Notably, Shopify, the Canadian e-commerce leader, was added to the top 10 and is one of the few that does not pay a dividend.

Figure 2: Top 10 Holdings

Sector Exposures

The higher beta observed (1.11) is reflected in over-weight allocations in the more volatile Consumer Discretionary (+10%) and Information Technology (+6%) sectors, and a 5% under-weight in the low-vol Consumer Staples sector.

Consistent with the previous methodology, the Index remains under-weight in Financials (~7%). The return-on-assets screen continues to penalize the highly levered banking sector that heavily populates ex-U.S. markets.

Figure 3: Sector Exposures

Country Exposures

A unique enhancement of the new methodology is regional scoring and selection within defined geographic segments. This adjustment brings regional allocations closer to the underlying market-cap universe.

Regional exposures after the rebalance:

  • Europe exposure remains largely unchanged
  • Emerging Markets decreased by 8%
  • Developed ex-Europe increased by nearly 9%

Figure 4: Regional Exposures

Top country exposure changes after the rebalance:

  • Top increases: China (+10%) and Canada (+7%)
  • Top decreases: Taiwan, the United Kingdom and India (each down ~6%–7%)

Figure 5: Top Country Changes

More Directly Targeting Large Growth

The updated methodology focuses on a narrower, higher-quality set of 200 large-cap growth companies drawn from a broader global universe, unconstrained by the dividend requirement.

Key Takeaways:

  • Larger-cap tilt: 99% large caps (vs. 79% previously)
  • Lower dividend yield: 1.5% (vs. 2.5%) due to removal of dividend requirement
  • Higher valuations: P/E 25x (vs. 22x), reflecting stronger growth bias
  • Improved profitability: ROE 20.2% (vs. 17.9%)
  • Minimal exposure to unprofitable companies: <1%

Figure 6: WisdomTree Global ex-U.S. Quality Growth Index Before and After

Comparison to MSCI ACWI ex-USA Growth Index

Following the rebalance, the WisdomTree Global ex-U.S. Quality Growth Index shows a 15% higher holdings overlap with the MSCI ACWI ex-USA Growth Index. This results in a lower ex ante tracking error and a beta of 1.11, aligning DNL more directly with global growth benchmarks.

Figure 7: Index Comparison

Rebalance Turnover

Turnover for this year's rebalance was 67% (one-way)—up 11 percentage points from 2024, but in line with levels seen between 2021 and 2022.

Figure 8: Historical Rebalance Turnover

Positioning for the Next Phase of Global Growth

With this evolution, DNL transitions from a dividend-driven framework to a pure quality growth strategy.

By aligning with the U.S. Quality Growth framework, DNL will soon offer investors a cohesive global quality growth solution—balancing strong fundamentals, consistent profitability and exposure to the world's leading growth franchises outside the United States.

This repositioning strengthens DNL's relevance for investors seeking long-term capital appreciation through a disciplined, fundamentals-based approach to quality and growth.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal.

DNL: Growth stocks, as a group, may be out of favor with the market and underperform value stocks or the overall equity market. Growth stocks are generally more sensitive to market movements than other types of stocks. The Fund is non-diversified; as a result, changes in the market value of a single security could cause greater fluctuations in the value of Fund shares than would occur in a diversified fund. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. To the extent the Fund invests a significant portion of its assets in securities of companies of a single country or region, it is more likely to be impacted by events or conditions affecting that country or region. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit. The Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets, and the Index may not perform as intended.

QGRW: Growth stocks, as a group, may be out of favor with the market and underperform value stocks or the overall equity market. Growth stocks are generally more sensitive to market movements than other types of stocks. The Fund is non-diversified; as a result, changes in the market value of a single security could cause greater fluctuations in the value of Fund shares than would occur in a diversified fund. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit. The Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets, and the Index may not perform as intended.

DGRW: Funds focusing their investments in certain sectors increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time.

Please read each Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Matt Wagner, CFA, Director, Research

Matt Wagner joined WisdomTree in May 2017 as an Analyst on the Research team. He currently serves as a Director, where he supports the creation, maintenance, and reconstitution of WisdomTree’s indexes and actively managed ETFs. Matt began his career at Morgan Stanley, working as an analyst in Treasury Capital Markets from 2015 to 2017, focusing on unsecured funding planning, execution, and risk management. He graduated magna cum laude from Boston College in 2015 with a B.A. in International Studies, concentrating in Economics. In 2020, he earned a Certificate in Advanced Valuation from NYU Stern. He is also a Chartered Financial Analyst (CFA) charterholder.
