data.title: Energy Transfer: My Favorite In The U.S. Energy Sector
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The modern boom in artificial intelligence is driving growth in the number of projects related to the launch of data centers. This is increasing electricity consumption, which makes its production important, with gas accounting for 42% of the total share of resources used. New data from the EIA shows that electricity demand in the US economy will increase to 4,305 billion kWh in 2026. This dynamic creates an extremely optimistic outlook for Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET), which is a major pipeline operator transporting gas to LNG terminals.

With its competitive advantages in the form of a diversified pipeline network, stable cash flows, and ability to reduce operating costs through project scaling, Energy Transfer is an attractive investment asset with growth still ahead. Meanwhile, an important condition for the development of their business is the huge undistributed profit, which enables the financing of new infrastructure projects. And the dividend yield of about 8% and the MLP tax specification make these securities attractive for long-term investments with the aim of obtaining high passive income.

Note: A key advantage of MLPs is that dividend payments are considered a return of capital rather than income, thus reducing taxation for individual investors who are US residents (for non-US residents, there are specific rules that limit the benefits of investing in MLP assets). Read more about the features and tax principles of investing in energy sector MLPs at this link.

Energy Transfer's Strategy And Growth Drivers

Energy Transfer is a major pipeline operator transporting gas to LNG terminals. Its pipelines have a total length of 140,000 miles. Despite being privately owned, it is important to note that the company is highly dependent on the state. This is due to the fact that its large-scale infrastructure crosses the borders of most states. Consequently, the construction and operation of the pipeline network is subject to strict federal government regulation.

A feature of Energy Transfer's business model is that its revenue is generated mainly from commissions. Based on the latest financial statements, approximately 90% of the company's revenue consists of commission payments. The structure of revenues shows a balanced distribution between sources of cash flow. The main part (27% of revenues) comes from the supply of liquefied natural gas and petroleum products. Around 21% of revenues are commissions for domestic and international transportation. Crude oil and natural gas transportation account for 20% and 19%, respectively. The other 13% of the company's revenue comes from a bunch of affiliates and subsidiaries, like Sunoco and USA Compression.

The formation of Energy Transfer's current organizational management structure at MPL took place on October 19, 2018. This change was a good thing for the company, as it set the stage for strategic growth. It is primarily related to the fact that ET can transfer its annual asset depreciation to an increase in distributions, which counts as a return of capital. It makes the company's tax management more efficient. But, at the same time, investing in ET has limitations for investors. It restricts the number of participants among shareholders and also forms a negative discount when assessing the investment attractiveness of securities compared to the assets of competing organizations.

Based on the fact that the majority of Energy Transfer's revenue consists of commission fees, it has low dependence on market volatility in crude oil, natural gas, and liquefied natural gas prices. Low hydrocarbon prices remain a serious challenge in 2025 for most companies involved in the development of the US oil and gas industry. This is supported by EIA forecasts, which, besides growth in demand for electricity, predict an increase in the price of liquefied natural gas, while oil prices are expected to decline.

That is why the main price risk lies with crude oil. Since its share in ET's revenue structure is 19%, this threat factor is minimized. The main revenue of the company comes from gas transportation. But the price situation there is completely different. Since Q1 2024, there has been a recovery in Natural gas price at Henry Hub quotations (from $1.52 per million British thermal units). As we already know, according to EIA forecasts, this growth should continue until the end of 2026.

Growth catalysts for Energy Transfer include the current demand for natural gas and liquefied natural gas in the US and abroad. Demand is expected to increase due to the impact of the technological boom in the development of artificial intelligence technologies. The ET Assets include approximately 105,000 miles of natural gas interstate and intrastate pipelines, approximately 236 Bcf of natural gas storage, and expertise in dual-drive gas/electrical compression.

Expected construction of new data centers supports the realism of EIA forecasts regarding growth in electricity demand in the US to 4,305 billion kWh in 2026. This provides incentives for projects to build new power plants, where, although the share of gas is expected to decline to 40% in 2026, this hydrocarbon will still play an important role.

The potential for expanding LNG exports deserves special attention. This is due to the fact that the role of gas in electricity generation will remain stable not only in the US but also in other regions of the world. By 2040, experts expect global demand for liquefied natural gas to grow by 60%, reaching between 630 and 718 million tons per year. Among the key factors supporting this forecast are the increasing influence of artificial intelligence on the construction of data centers and the environmental policies of countries and major industrial companies to reduce CO2 emissions into the atmosphere.

In order to understand the role of these trends in the global LNG market for Energy Transfer's development prospects, it should be noted that Europe will remain the main consumer of this resource. This is related to the need to compensate for the share of intermittent renewable energy sources in electricity production for European consumers and industrialists. Considering that CO2 emissions from the use of liquefied natural gas are the lowest among traditional hydrocarbons, demand for this commodity will remain high. In order to ensure LNG supplies to Europe, work will be carried out with the major pipeline operator. By 2030, US companies are expected to supply around 180 million tons of LNG per year, which will account for 30% to 35% of the global share.

The potential for ET to participate in addressing requests related to increasing the export capacity of the US liquefied natural gas sector is possible through the implementation of new infrastructure projects that enable the transportation of LNG to export terminals, from which the product is then delivered to end customers around the world. For instance, this potential can be realized through the conversion of the Lake Charles project, which would be aimed at exporting approximately 16.5 million metric tons of LNG per year. Chevron, China's ENN Energy, and South Korea's SK Gas Trading are among the customers that have signed long-term contracts to purchase LNG from the Lake Charles terminal. Due to rising costs, however, a final investment decision is expected to be made in Q1 2026.

Energy Transfer Financial Profile

Based on the info in the Q2 2025 report, Energy Transfer had an adjusted EBITDA of $3.9 billion. Its mix is: adjusted EBITDA from liquefied natural gas and petroleum products of $1 billion, from production and refining of $768 million, from crude oil of $732 million, from interstate natural gas of $470 million, and from domestic natural gas of $284 million.

The Energy Transfer uses its asset portfolio to generate balanced and efficient profits in such a way that no single business segment accounts for more than one-third of consolidated adjusted EBITDA. Additionally, approximately 40% of Energy Transfer's adjusted EBITDA is attributable to natural gas-related assets. The company's detailed financial results are shown below. The key indicators allow Energy Transfer's financial stability to be assessed as high, increasing the investment attractiveness of its shares.

ET Stock Valuation

As Energy Transfer is an MLP, its investment attractiveness is influenced by the specifics of its organizational management structure. That is why ET is currently trading at lower multiples than its competitors in the sector. This is caused by the complex structure of MLPs and lower liquidity on the stock market (because of the lack of access to ET securities by some funds and the low share of foreign investors who do not want to deal with the peculiarities of K-1 tax reporting).

Speaking of the traditional price/earnings ratio, Energy Transfer's P/E GAAP (TTM) is 12.94, and P/E GAAP (FWD) is 12.05. That's 5.91% and 7.35% less than the sector median, respectively. So, there's not really any competitive advantage in ET's valuation here.

Analyzing Energy Transfer's profitability, it's worth noting that analysts' consensus forecast for Q3 2025 is EPS of $0.33, which is $0.01 more than for the same period in 2024. The growth should be 3.85%, while in Q4, analysts expect EPS to grow by 25.58% (y/y).

More attractive assessment of the investment appeal of ET securities by Wall Street analysts, which historically has always been high for this asset. Based on the data as of October 22, 2025, 12 analysts give a "Strong Buy" recommendation, 5 analysts give a "Buy" recommendation, and only 1 analyst remains neutral on ET, recommending to "Hold." Wall Street analysts give a rating of 4.61, while Seeking Alpha experts give a rating of 4.33. These high scores demonstrate the overall commitment of professional market participants and finance experts that Energy Transfer shares should be added to their investment portfolios.

One important reason for recommending ET shares as a "Buy" is their high dividend yield, which is currently 7.87% on a forward basis. This is 79.36% higher than the sector median (4.39%). It should be noted that since Energy Transfer is an MLP, shareholders receive payments (as part of the distribution) instead of dividends. This is an attractive investment for investors who are focused on generating passive income. There are a number of reasons for this. Firstly, there is a tax deferral mechanism. Thanks to this, the investor's tax base is reduced. The tax deferral is valid until the share is sold or the tax base is zeroed. Often, ET shares are purchased with the aim of holding them and receiving income until retirement, when income tax rates may be lower.

Another important thing to note is that the ET dividend yield is affected by interest rates. With the Fed's monetary policy expected to keep changing, we should expect a long-term cycle of interest rate cuts in the foreseeable future. Next meeting is on October 29, 2025, and the Fed is expected to cut rates by 25 basis points (to 4.0%). Meanwhile, by the end of 2026, the central bank is expected to cut interest rates at least four times, down to 3.0%. There is a 61.8% probability of such a decision. To play on the decline in interest rates by receiving high dividends, for example, around 16%, I recently recommended another security—Dynex Capital (DX). The Energy Transfer is another contender for high dividend yields, the level of which may be higher due to the Fed's interest rate cut.

Risks Of The Investment Thesis

As my recommendation for ET is "Buy," the risks of the investment thesis directly relate to threat factors that could impact the effectiveness of Energy Transfer’s business model and business strategy. As part of the company’s conference call following the presentation of its Q2 2025 quarterly results, management identified the following key risks: weakness of the Bakken field, high market volatility, delays in the commercialization of data center projects, competition from other LNG pipelines, and legal risks related to right of way and ownership.

I think the main risks are tied to the macroeconomic situation in the US and around the world. Firstly, this will determine the actual demand for liquefied natural gas and electricity. The forecasted growth in demand is linked to expectations surrounding the development of the artificial intelligence sector and data centers. However, this segment of technology services is directly dependent on general macroeconomic processes. In case of factors that threaten the likelihood of a recession in the US economy, all investment ideas that are expected to be affected by artificial intelligence and growth in demand for electricity may be revised. The important countermeasure on the part of ET is that 90% of its revenue comes from commission income. That is why factors such as falling prices for natural gas and crude oil will have a more minimal impact than on other competing companies in this sector.

Conclusion

In summary, the results of my fundamental analysis of Energy Transfer shares allow me to evaluate them as an asset worth adding to today's investment portfolio. The main factors include high dividend yield, stable revenue (90% of which consists of commission income), expectations for the implementation of projects to build new data centers during the artificial intelligence boom, and positive forecasts for growth in demand for liquefied natural gas, its transportation, and electricity production in the US.

All of these triggers indicate the high investment attractiveness of ET shares. Considering that their yield will remain potentially even higher during the period of the Fed's interest rate cuts, this type of financial instrument should be of particular interest to investors who are interested in maximizing passive income in the long term, with the possibility of obtaining a tax deferral at the same time.

data.title: Ferrari: Guidance For 2030 Sets The Stage For Outperformance
data.description:
I rate Ferrari stock (NYSE:RACE) (BIT:RACE) as a "strong buy," as I see the recent drop as a long-term opportunity to build an important position in a high-quality business for a "buy and hold" strategy. The guidance for 2030 has disappointed investors who might anticipate lower demand for Ferrari's luxury sports cars; however, I think investors are mistaken, because Ferrari, unlike other carmakers, does not depend significantly on demand to support its future growth, and its pricing power is strong. Under very conservative revenue guidance for 2030, management has complete control to significantly exceed those expectations. I believe there are valid reasons for setting a very conservative revenue target for 2030, and these reasons may not be connected to a decrease in demand for Ferrari's cars.

In this context, outperformance refers not merely to Ferrari beating its stated 2030 targets, but to delivering earnings growth, margin expansion, and shareholder returns above these conservative projections. The combination of brand exclusivity, hybrid-led pricing leverage, and disciplined capital allocation offers Ferrari a favorable upside-to-downside opportunity: limited downside if macro conditions tighten, but meaningful upside if management gradually monetizes its pricing and customization power.

I was following up on the stock some years ago, and I considered it expensive then, but now, with the recent drop, having fallen 30% from its all-time high, I started accumulating shares to complete a position that weighs 5% of my entire stock portfolio at an average cost of 327 euros per share. I am keen to expand the weight of my position to 12%.

This is a truly unique company whose competitive advantages are most likely linked to a very strong brand, which gives Ferrari strong pricing power. I like these types of companies because they are very hard to disrupt since a strong brand cannot be disrupted by technology or AI. I am holding another company with similar characteristics and pricing power, Hermès (OTCPK:HESAY)(OTCPK:HESAF), from which I've written an extensive article explaining the source of its moat. You can check it out to have on your radar another outstanding company.

Now, I will briefly explain my bullish thesis of Ferrari to help you understand why I bought shares of this company, taking advantage of this recent drop. Let's go.

Context

In Q2 2025, Ferrari's revenues only grew 4.4% YoY, reaching 1.79 billion, which was under the expectation of 1.82 billion euros. In addition, Ferrari also slightly missed EPS expectations, as it was 2.38 euros when the market expected 2.40 euros per share. In Q2, Ferrari managed to achieve an operating margin of 30.9% compared to that of Q2 2024 of 29.9%.

In general, these results were very decent ones despite the fact that Ferrari missed the consensus slightly. If we mention the accumulated results, taking H1 2025 to compare it with H1 2024, Ferrari's revenues grew 8.5% YoY, whereas operating profit and net income grew 14.8% YoY and 9.4% YoY, respectively.

These results reinforce the idea that Ferrari will surpass its guidance for the FY 2025 without many problems:

In the table, you can see that, for FY2025, Ferrari expects a revenue growth higher than 5%, an adjusted EPS growth of more than 2%, and industrial FCF growth of more than 17%. The industrial FCF is the FCF excluding the company's financial services to its clients, such as leasing, loans, balloon payments, tailor-made financial packages for limited series models, etc., in order to focus only on the core manufacturing business.

Expectations For Q3 FY2025

Consensus expects revenues of 1.72 billion euros for Q3 2025, which would mean a fall of -3.6% compared to the previous quarter in FY 2025 and a growth of 4.6% YoY. Regarding the EPS, consensus expects 2.07 euros per share for Q3, which would mean a fall of -13% and a growth of -0.53% YoY.

While Ferrari confirmed its FY 2025 guidance and reiterated its 2030 strategic targets, investors should watch for transitional signals in Q3 2025 that indicate how well the company is executing its hybrid-to-electric shift without margin dilution. Key areas include product-mix profitability, free cash flow resilience amid elevated capex, and the order pipeline’s depth as the first fully electric model nears launch.

A Unique Business Focused On Exclusivity, Preserving The Brand

When we talk about Ferrari, we are not talking about a typical carmaker that manufactures lots of models and volumes while competing with other brands basically in price, innovations, and services. A typical carmaker does not have a strong brand advantage in their respective segments, so their pricing power is limited, which is offset by higher volumes of cars sold.

For instance, if you want to buy a limited-edition car or hypercar right away, you can't, but you can buy "entry models" such as a Ferrari Roma, Roma Spider, or Ferrari Portofino to start a new relationship with the brand. Now, the problem with this beginning is that even those "entry models" have a lot of demand, so you would be included in a waiting list that can last between 12 and 36 months.

Now, to have the chance to buy the "entry models," it is very important that you buy them from an official Ferrari dealership, because if you don't, you could face issues with future purchase requests, particularly if you want to own a limited edition model in the future. Ferrari does a thorough background check on all its potential customers, as they want to make sure that you are genuinely passionate about the brand, that you are financially solid, and that you will not make modifications in absurd ways, among other things that might negatively impact the brand.

Once you place your order, you'll need to choose color, trim, materials, carbon, wheels, etc., by visiting Ferrari Atelier or being part of the Tailor Made program. Normally, you can wait between 6 and 8 months, depending on the model and demand, but for ultra-custom, one-off models, it's possible that you might wait for 4 or 5 years.

In addition, once you've completed the process of the purchase, you need to establish a long-term relationship with the official dealers while being part of clients' exclusive events and programs carefully designed to enable clients to experience the true spirit of the brand. In other words, you cannot resell your new Ferrari, as that would go against the loyalty to the brand that the company wants to develop in each new client.

Heritage, technology, and racing are three pillars behind the brand that enable Ferrari to construct this aura of exclusivity among its loyal clients; indeed, these three pillars are very hard to replicate by other brands, so the result is that since 1947, when Ferrari started its manufacturing of cars, at least 90% of all those cars produced since then are still alive, as was mentioned by an executive of the company on the Investor Day in October 2025:

Since the start of our activities back in 1947, we have been introducing 330,000 cars in the market. And we know that much more than 90% are still alive in the market and require our constant attention.

That reveals that those pillars are not just words, but they are truly instilled in each Ferrari client to keep a long-term relationship with the brand.

Seeing all the intangibles associated with the brand, I am not surprised by the solid financial performance shown in the table above in the last 8 years. The company was growing at a double-digit rate in most of the years, with solid gross margins higher than 50%, decent FCF margins, acceptable debt levels despite its nature of being a heavy-asset company, and very good returns on capital.

For being a carmaker, those results are excellent, but let's see why the stock price dropped recently to see if it's justified or not.

Guidance For 2030: Analysts Disappointed

Analysts were disappointed as soon as Ferrari's management announced revenues of 9 billion euros for 2030, which means a growth of only around 5% annually for the upcoming years; this growth clearly is lower than what the consensus was expecting.

Also, there was more disappointment when Ferrari announced that its product mix will be 40% internal combustion engine (ICE), 40% hybrid, and 20% fully electric (EVs) for 2030.

The 20% EV target is a clear reduction from a previous target of 40% EVs by 2030 from earlier plans. In my view, the only "issue" from all these announcements would be the lower-than-expected revenue growth for 2030, but even that is not necessarily bad news; I'll explain it later.

It's positive to have a 30% operating profit margin for 2030, a robust cumulated industrial FCF (2026-2030) of around 8 billion euros, from which 7 billion euros will be used to reward shareholders through share buybacks and dividends. I think that analysts overreacted in their disappointment, viewing Ferrari as any other carmaker whose reduction in guidance meant an actual lower expected demand for its cars.

The Ferrari's revenue growth mainly comes from the control in the production and higher average selling prices (ASP), which include not only the price of the cars but also all the services associated with the cars, such as customization, spare part sales, and other related services.

As you can see, the Ferrari's ASP is increasing gradually over time, which protects the margins in scenarios of higher input costs, more tariffs, more enforced regulations, etc. In this sense, I do not see the fact that Ferrari lowers its EV targets as something bad; in fact, I see a management that does not want to take more risks entering strongly into a sector that is outside Ferrari's circle of competence.

In my view, the guidance reduction is not a matter of lack of ambition, as some analysts might think; after all, we are talking about a company that is committed to not increasing significantly the production to strengthen its exclusivity. On the other hand, it is not a lack of demand either, as the demand for these cars is always higher than production; that's why customers still need to be part of waiting lists.

Lower Regulations And Slow Entry Into The EV Sector Might Be The Explanation For The Reduced Guidance

I was wondering what would have happened if Ferrari had kept its previous target of 40% in EVs for 2030; furthermore, if Ferrari had given a forecast of a more ambitious revenue growth for 2030, such as 12% annual growth, that would have sounded way better, and even the stock price would have appreciated the day of the announcement.

Nevertheless, that target of 12% annual growth would mean revenues of 12.7 billion euros for 2030, from which 5.1 billion euros would come from EVs. Just to picture the number, those 5.1 billion euros from EVs would represent 71% of the estimated total revenues for 2025. That's totally unrealistic and too risky to achieve, as Ferrari would need to dedicate more and more resources to fulfill that promise, particularly that related to the revenues for EVs, which is a sector that requires further assessment to penetrate it with a clear and smart strategy.

So, Ferrari might have decided to lower expectations, not only reducing the total revenue growth to around 5% per year but also lowering the target for EVs from 40% to 20%, which means total revenues of 9 billion euros for 2030, from which only 1.8 billion euros would be the target for EVs in 2030. That sounds more reasonable and achievable.

In addition, I think that maybe Ferrari not only was assessing the feasibility of the 40% target for EVs, including a high revenue growth for 2030, but also the management might have studied the possibility that a very ambitious target could have attracted the eyes of regulators to reduce Ferrari's current exemptions.

Indeed, given its status as a Small Volume Manufacturer (SMV) in some jurisdictions, such as the EU, US, etc., Ferrari benefits from certain exemptions and less stringent emission caps compared to a larger volume manufacturer. In the EU, an SMV status is granted when the manufacturer produces less than 10,000 vehicles in the EU per year.

In the US, Ferrari also qualifies as an SMV under the EPA GHG rules, which require the manufacturer to produce less than 5,000 vehicles in that jurisdiction. In this sense, all these exemptions give Ferrari breathing room, as it may not be forced to convert all its models to EV by some regulatory deadlines. This would also mean more flexibility to keep producing ICE and hybrid technology.

Of course, Ferrari is not free from some regulations, such as those related to emissions, safety, CO₂ fleet average rules, etc., but those exemptions certainly reduce the pressure to make significant and expensive changes in the production processes and planning.

As such, in my point of view, Ferrari's management gave responsible guidance for 2030 with a clear shareholder orientation while carefully preserving the brand's reputation.

Now, we are going to see the valuation, in which I will show that the current stock price is very decent to start a position now.

Valuation

According to SA, the valuation grade is F, which clearly indicates that the stock is more expensive than the comparables of its sector in each valuation multiple. Now, we need to take into account that the comparables might be very different from Ferrari's business model; most likely, many of them do not have Ferrari's pricing power, or Ferrari's exclusivity associated with its brands. Certainly, those comparables might face fierce competition in their sectors, which is not the situation of Ferrari.

However, if we compare each multiple with its respective average of the last 5 years, we find that in almost every multiple valuation, Ferrari is trading now lower than its average multiple of the last 5 years.

However, I would like to see a range of the intrinsic value under certain assumptions to see clearly why the stock is a long-term buy. As such, I propose the following method, but first, I will make some assumptions:

  • Outstanding shares: 179.9 million (-0.79% annual decline of outstanding shares due to share buybacks taking the average of the last 8 years).

  • FCF margins: 17% (in the last 2 years, FCF margins were around 14%, but the guidance for the cumulated industrial FCF of 8 billion euros could mean $7 billion of cumulated FCF for the period 2026-30, which would mean a 17% FCF margin for that projected period).

  • Historically, in Ferrari's case, the FCF was lower than the industrial FCF by 5-10% in average in the last years, so that's why I assumed a cumulated FCF of around 7 billion euros for the period 2026-2030.

  • Revenue growth: according to guidance for 2030 (4.8% annual growth until 2030).

  • Discounted rate: 8% (It's one of the best businesses in the world, so a discounted rate of 8% sounds reasonable).

  • P/FCF estimated at 60.4x, which is an 8-year average.

I take the revenues projected for 2030, and then I multiply those revenues by the FCF margin of 17%. As a result, I get an approximate FCF of 1,537 million euros for 2030.

Now, I take that FCF of 1,537 million to be divided by the outstanding number of shares of 179.9 million shares to get an FCF per share for 2030, getting 8.91. In this sense, I take the FCF per share of 8.91 and multiply it by 60.4x. Then, I get a target price of 537.9 dollars per share in 2030, so I calculate the present value to bring it back to 2025.

Thus, finding the intrinsic value:

Intrinsic value = 537.9 / (1 + discounted rate)^6.

Finally, I get an approximate intrinsic value of 338.9 euros per share, so apparently there is no room for upside here since the stock price is at around 342-344 euros per share.

However, the projection of the revenues until 2030 has implicit assumptions in two components: 1) the projected growth in the number of cars until 2030, and 2) the growth of ASP until 2030, so I'll show them more clearly in order to change them and see how the intrinsic value can change (for simplicity, all the other sources of Ferrari's revenues, such as customizations and others, are included in the projected ASP):

Here, you can see that in the past 8 years, the average ASP growth was 5% per year, whereas the average growth in the number of cars was 7.3% per year, resulting in an average of 12% annual growth in total revenues.

Now, according to the guidance for 2030, 9 billion euros of revenue would mean an ASP annual growth of 4% and an annual growth in the number of cars of 0.8% until 2030. As such, I changed both variables to see how the intrinsic value could change, making a sensitivity analysis; these are the results:

In the table above, you can see different combinations of the number of cars growth and ASP growth; in the first cell, we see the combination between these two variables that is assumed in the guidance for 2030, which results in an intrinsic value of 338.9 euros per share that I've shown before.

Now, if we focus on the combination of a number of cars growth of 7%, and an ASP growth of 5%, which incidentally is the combination achieved by Ferrari in the last 8 years, the intrinsic value can reach 479 euros per share. That would mean an upside of 40%, considering a current stock price of around 342 euros per share.

This upside means that Ferrari would surpass its targets for 2030 relatively easy, and I think the company will achieve it, as it has full control of its production with so much room to keep setting higher prices for its cars. Few companies in the world have this privileged position to raise prices without negatively impacting their demand. That's why I consider Ferrari a clear buy as a long-term holding after the recent drop in the stock price.

To summarize all the facts and information presented, my bullish thesis is based on the fact that Ferrari announced a very conservative target for 2030 to surpass it gradually quarter by quarter, to have more flexibility in the further development of its EV cars, and to avoid sending a wrong message to regulators.

Risks

One risk frequently mentioned in Ferrari is the fact that the ASP could grow lower than expected, depending on the mix of prices between discontinued cars and new cars. This situation would be more difficult to manage in the short term, but way more feasible to manage over longer periods of time. This is a challenge managed by the company over decades, showing that it knows how to phase in and phase out models profitably.

Another risk is that regulators will enforce new and more rules in the upcoming years for manufacturers under the SMV status, like Ferrari. This is something that might happen, but I like that Ferrari is looking to lower that chance, for example, by taking a more conservative forecast as one way to not call the attention of regulators while showing very conservative guidance for 2030, which implicitly means an average number of cars growth of only 0.8% annually, as we've seen before, reinforcing its current Ferrari status of SMV.

A third risk is that Ferrari was not able to make a successful transition toward EVs; in other words, that Ferrari's EVs are not recognized by customers as luxury sports cars that encompass the main values that are in any ICE or hybrid Ferrari. Nevertheless, I am confident that the management has acknowledged this potential risk, so the guidance for 2030 was reduced to take a more gradual step in this segment.

Now, some investors might think that the automotive sector is required to manufacture full EVs by 2035 in the EU. However, Ferrari is not competing with mass-volume automakers, and the regulation specifically acknowledges SMVs, or niche manufacturers, allowing derogations for them such as fleet-average targets, special treatment, etc. Thus, Ferrari has important advantages due to its SMV status, which gives the company more flexibility in adapting the regulation impact into its product strategy.

Takeaways

Ferrari is a company whose moat is based entirely on its brand, from which heritage, innovation, and race are the pillars behind that brand. Ferrari makes sure that any customer truly understands that being part of Ferrari's ecosystem is a privilege that should be taken care of over time. I do not know any other brand as strong as Ferrari within the automotive industry in which I would put my money.

I am a very conservative long-term investor who considers that the automotive sector in general is a very hard sector with high capital expenditures, lots of competition, and relatively low pricing power, and whose brands are not strong enough to protect them from competition, so most of the players in the industry need to invest in more and more innovations combined with a limited pricing power, which makes it harder to sustain the margins over time.

Ferrari is one of those few exceptions, a very unique company in the automotive sector whose moat is entirely linked to its powerful brand being a low-volume, exclusive sports car manufacturer, which makes it resilient to potential threats in the industry, such as regulations, macroeconomic turmoils, competition, etc. In all these threats, Ferrari can just set higher prices, and the demand will not be impacted materially.

As such, if you are seeking opportunities that are very hard to disrupt by AI over the long haul, you need to consider Ferrari, whose brand is so powerful and whose customers are so loyal that no matter how much technology or AI competitors want to offer, they will never convince them to leave an exclusive and traditional brand like Ferrari to buy the cars offered by competitors, even if they are exclusive too.

In my view, there is no better resistance to AI or any other technological disruption to a powerful brand; Apple (NYSE: AAPL), Hermes, or Ferrari are just examples of rare companies that are well protected from the disruption risk since their moats are linked to their brands, which instill emotions and loyalty in their customers toward those brands.


Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best AI-Resistant Idea investment competition, which runs through November 10.
